professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: More and more online retailers now surround their products with urgency cues — ticking countdown timers, "only two left in stock" banners, and "twelve people are viewing this" pop-ups. Some marketers defend these tactics as honest nudges that help indecisive shoppers finally act, while others say they manufacture false pressure that pushes people into purchases they later regret. Should companies be allowed to use these artificial urgency tactics? Why or why not?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I believe these tactics cross the line into manipulation. A countdown clock that quietly resets on every visit isn't information; it is engineered pressure designed to bypass careful thinking. Shoppers deserve to judge a purchase on its actual merits, not on a manufactured fear of missing out.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: In my opinion, a measured amount of urgency is a legitimate part of selling. Genuine low stock and real deadlines do exist, and reminding customers of them can be a useful service rather than a trick. The problem is dishonesty, not urgency itself, so I would regulate false claims instead of banning the technique.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been discussing how schools organize students for instruction this semester. A number of districts sort children into separate classes by measured ability — a practice often called tracking — for core subjects like math and reading. Supporters say this lets teachers pitch each lesson at the right level, while critics argue it quietly lowers expectations for anyone placed in a lower group. Should schools group students by ability in this way?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think ability grouping ends up doing more harm than good. Once a child is labeled and placed in a lower track, teachers and the students themselves often stop expecting much, and that self-fulfilling prophecy is hard to escape. Mixed classrooms let those who struggle learn alongside stronger peers.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: In my opinion, grouping by ability is simply the practical choice. A single teacher cannot meaningfully reach both a student who has already mastered the material and one who is still lost, and aiming for the middle bores the advanced while overwhelming the behind. Careful, flexible placement serves everyone better.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: A hiring manager can now type an applicant's name into a search bar and scroll through years of personal posts before the two ever meet. Some firms treat this as basic due diligence that protects a company's reputation, while others insist that judging candidates on off-hours photos and opinions is an invasion of privacy with no bearing on job performance. Should employers factor personal social media into hiring decisions?

students.name: Kelly
students.text: Screening someone's private social media has no real place in hiring. What a person posts about their weekend or their politics rarely reveals whether they can do the job, yet a single old joke taken out of context can quietly cost them an offer. That kind of power is far too easy to abuse.

students.name: Paul
students.text: A company has every right to understand who it is about to employ. Public posts are exactly that — public — and a visible pattern of dishonesty or cruelty online is fair warning about how someone might treat coworkers and clients. Ignoring freely available information would be naive, not principled.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: We've been examining how living traditions change once outsiders take an interest in them. In many towns, centuries-old festivals have been rescheduled, shortened, and staged for visiting tourists, bringing in money that helps the community survive. Some anthropologists welcome this as a way to keep a fading custom alive, while others warn that a ritual performed for ticket holders slowly loses its real meaning. Should communities reshape their traditions to attract tourists?

students.name: Paul
students.text: I believe adapting traditions for tourism is often a fair trade. The income can fund the very costumes, instruments, and gatherings that would otherwise vanish as young people leave for the cities. A festival that bends a little in order to survive still passes something genuine to the next generation.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: Tourist money tends to hollow out the very thing it claims to be saving. Once a ceremony is scheduled around bus tours and photo opportunities, its sacred timing gets rearranged for strangers, and what remains is a performance rather than a lived belief. Some traditions are worth shielding from the market.
